This correspondence is defined as follows: if V is a simple unitarizable (g, K)q-module then it has a unique invariant inner product up to a scalar and Rq(g, K) acts on it through bounded operators, its completion ¯V is then an irreducible representation of C∗ q (G); conversely, if H is an irreducible representation of C∗ q (G) then Rq(g, K)H is a simple (g, K)q-module. The quantization of the motion group. Let G⋆ 0 be the transformation Lie groupoid (see [27, §1.2.a]) of the right action of K on K\U and let b be the base point of K\U. It is an amenable groupoid and its C*-algebra C∗(G⋆ 0) coincides with the crossed product K ⋉C(K\U). By comparison, the group C*-algebra of the motion group G0 is naturally isomorphic to K ⋉C0((g/k)ˆ). As we will see, as the Mackey parameter approaches 0, the family of quantizations of G converges (at the C*-algebraic level) to the deformation to the normal cone [15] induced by the embedding K ,→G⋆ 0, where K is identified with the subgroup(oid) of elements of G⋆ 0 having source and range equal to the base point b of K\U. The representation theoretic analogy between G and G0 can then be extended to the q-deformations of G, provided that we replace G0 by G⋆ 0.

The continuous field of C*-algebras The aim of this third section is to define the continuous field of C*-algebras evoked in the introduction. In order to have a better insight into this construction, we first define an algebraic field for (the analogues of) the universal enveloping ∗-algebras. More precisely, we construct an involutive algebra AU(g) defined over the ring A of analytic functions on R∗ + × R and we show that it specializes at each (q, t) ∈R∗ + × R according to the following table. q ̸= 1 q = 1 t ̸= 0 Uqt(g) U(g) t = 0 U(g⋆ 0) U(g0) Although we do not prove it explicitly, the continuous field of C*-algebras is then constructed so that the elements of AU(g) give continuous vector fields of unbounded multipliers. 3.1. The algebraic quantum Mackey field. In this subsection, we define the algebra AU(g) and show how some of its modules specialize at points (q, t) to the various irreducible representations introduced in Section 2. Let A be a commutative ∗-algebra, let φ : A →C be a ∗-algebra morphism and let B be a unital (A, ∗)-algebra, that is, a unital ∗-algebra equipped with a unital morphism of ∗-algebras ι : A →Z(B), where Z(B) denotes the center of B. The previous results suggest that the Hilbert completions of the modules indq,t �ε, ϙλ , for fixed (ε, λ) ∈{−1, 1}×iR, and D± q,t(n), for a given n ∈Z>0, should form continuous families of representations of a certain continuous field of C*-algebras C∗ r (G) over R∗ + × R with fibers (15) C∗ r (G)q,t =          C∗ qt,r(G) (q ̸= 1, t ̸= 0) C∗(G⋆ 0) (q ̸= 1, t = 0) C∗ r (G) (q = 1, t ̸= 0) C∗(G0) (q = 1, t = 0). The aim of the present subsection is to construct this continuous field. Let us outline our strategy. First, we define a certain locally compact Hausdorff space S and equip it with an open continuous map L : S →R∗ + × R, called the location map. Roughly, the fiber Sq,t of L over a base point (q, t) parametrizes the (analogues) of the principal and discrete series associated to the deformation of G with parameter (q, t). Then, we define a locally constant continuous field of Hilbert spaces H over S and a constraint C on it. The Fourier transform will give rise to an embedding of C*-algebras C∗ r (G)q,t ,→KC (H)|Sq,t for each (q, t) ∈R∗ + × R. There exists a unique bijection µ from the spectrum of C∗ q,r(G) to the spectrum of C∗(G⋆ 0) such that for any irreducible representation ω of C∗ q,r(G), the representation α∗ 0ω of C∗(G⋆ 0) obtained by pullback by α0 contains µ(ω) as a subrepresentation. This map µ is continuous with respect to the Jacobson topologies, although its inverse is not. Proof. Assume that µ is a bijection Spec C∗ q,r(G) →Spec C∗(G⋆ 0) between the spec- tra of both algebras. Let us identify Spec C∗ q,r(G) with the list of irreducible uni- tarizable (g, K)q-modules given in Proposition 2.6. On the other hand, for any σ ∈{−1, 1} and n ∈Z≥0, we have α∗ 0[indq(1, σ)] = M m∈Zeven Ind(p⋆ σ, K, m), α∗ 0[D±(σ, n)] = M m−n∈Zodd ±m>n Ind(p⋆ σ, K, m). From this, an easy inductive argument using the bijectivity of µ implies shows that µ[indq(1, σ)] = Ind(p⋆ σ, K, 0), µ[D±(σ, n)] = Ind(p⋆ σ, K, ±(n + 1)). Conversely, one can check that the map thus defined satisfies the conditions of the theorem. Next, the continuity of µ derives from the following explicit descriptions of the Jacobson topology for Spec C∗ q,r(G) and Spec C∗(G⋆ 0). The topology of Spec C∗ q,r(G) is given by Corollary 1.8 via the isomorphism πq,1: the analogues of the discrete series are isolated, the set of analogues of the even principal series form a connected component homeomorphic to U+, while the irre- ducible analogues of the odd principal series form an open half-circle, the represen- tations D±(σ, 0) glued to its ends depending on σ. Similarly, since ResSc q,0 ◦πq,0 : C∗(G⋆ 0) →KC (H)|Sc q,0 is an isomorphism, see (16) and Proposition 3.12, we deduce from Corollary 1.8 an explicit description of Spec C∗(G⋆ 0). □ Remark 4.3. One can consider C∗(K) as the subalgebra of C∗(G⋆ 0) generated by the projections en, as n ranges over Z, and it can thus be embedded into C∗ q,r(G) through α0. This way, representations of C∗(G⋆ 0) or C∗ q,r(G) can be restricted to C∗(K). Let us identify the unitary dual of K = SO(2) with Z via n 7→ζn.
